Hi @uiri

I understand the hesitation. The decision is ultimately yours.

Using [pypinfo](https://github.com/ofek/pypinfo), we can check the statistics of downloads from PyPI from the last 30 days:

```bash
$ pypinfo --percent --markdown toml pyversion
```

| python_version | percent | download_count |
| -------------- | ------: | -------------: |
| 3.6            |  44.90% |        172,023 |
| 2.7            |  37.20% |        142,531 |
| 3.5            |   7.36% |         28,216 |
| 3.7            |   5.81% |         22,273 |
| 3.4            |   4.62% |         17,716 |
| 3.8            |   0.05% |            201 |
| 3.3            |   0.03% |            126 |
| 2.6            |   0.02% |             71 |

As you can see, Python 2.6 (and 3.3) numbers are very low. This is true for the [larger Python community as well](https://medium.com/@hugovk/python-version-share-over-time-cf4498822650).

Further, Python 2.6 is end of life. It is no longer receiving bug fixes, including for security issues. Python 2.6 went EOL on 2013-10-29 -- almost five years ago. For additional details on support Python versions, see:

https://devguide.python.org/#status-of-python-branches

Removing support for EOL Pythons will reduce testing and maintenance resources.

Here are some additional perspectives on why projects should drop Python 2.6 support from Python core developers:

- https://snarky.ca/stop-using-python-2-6/
- http://www.curiousefficiency.org/posts/2015/04/stop-supporting-python26.html

Here is an interesting overview of showing top Python projects and which ones have dropped Python 2.6 support, including [pip](https://pip.pypa.io/en/stable/news/):

https://hugovk.github.io/drop-python/2.6/

Projects that require 2.6 support can continue to use an old version of toml by pinning the dependency in their `requirements.txt` or `setup.py`.

Again, the decision is ultimately up to you and I'm fine either way, but I thought I'd share my reasons why I think it is a good idea.

Good points. The advantages/drawbacks of using a ``@cmdline`` approach are irrelevant from a library standpoint. Some people *will* code a ``@cmdline`` approach on top of Clize because it makes sense in their context. The question is thus: How can Clize make it easy for them to do just that?

Solutions could be:

1. Provide a ``cmdline`` decorator in Clize directly
2. Document a way to hook up a ``cmdline``-style approach into Clize
3. Pass module names to Clize ``run()`` instead of functions and have the logic look for ``@cmdline`` inside those modules. (ex: Django did a great job of making it possible to have random 3rd party app included in your project, all providing command line commands through manage.py).
4. Add a parameter to ``@cmdline`` that specifies the name of the ``__main__`` module under which the command is available (reverse dependency).

[Penn PoS tags](http://www.comp.leeds.ac.uk/amalgam/tagsets/upenn.html) define tokens by numbers with the tag CD, symbol tokens are tagged SYM for things like percentages, $ is used for currency tokens/symbols, and so forth.

In other words, the current behavior of `segtok` is the *correct* one and I am therefore closing this issue.

Fixed in furl v0.4.95, where a `ValueError` is raised if the host is egregiously
invalid.

```python
>>> import furl

>>> f = furl('https://user:pass@myhost.com/path/goes/here')

  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/furl/furl.py", line 1291, in __setattr__
    object.__setattr__(self, attr, value)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/furl/furl.py", line 930, in host
    raise ValueError(errmsg % host)
ValueError: Invalid host 'evil:hahaha@haxx0r.com'. Host strings must only contain [\.\-a-zA-Z0-9] and can't have adjacent periods.
```

Upgrade with `pip install furl -U`.

You actually can attach your inferior process to arbitrary terminals (ttys). To do this:

1. open a new terminal or terminal tab, run `tty`. It should output something like `/dev/pts/5` (`pts` is an emulated `tty`, which is fine). 

1. Run `set inferior-tty /dev/pts/5` [link](https://sourceware.org/gdb/onlinedocs/gdb/Input_002fOutput.html). You will prbably get the warning `&"warning: GDB: Failed to set controlling terminal: Operation not permitted\n"` but it seems to work fine for output. I'm not sure if it accepts input very well.

1. All i/o for the inferior process is then directed through that tty for the inferior program
 
Here is more more info.

> Often, I see nothing printed but it's actually waiting for my input
Unfortunately, when gdb is run as a subprocess, the kernel does not realize data is ready to be read until the program closes or the buffer is flushed. See [this thread](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/8251269/how-to-tell-gdb-to-flush-the-stdio-of-the-program-being-debugged) on stack overflow. 

I work around it in general by calling the `fflush` on the target program with the mi command `-data-evaluate-expression fflush(0)`  each time the program stops to force it to flush. If gdbgui doesn't know the program has stopped, it doesn't flush the buffer, and the user is left not knowing there is output, which is the problem you are describing.

In the scope of your foo function the only error ever raised is `RuntimeError`. You need to put the try block around the function itself as the retry decorator is raising the RetryError and not the original unwrapped foo function.

```
@tenacity.retry(stop=tenacity.stop_after_attempt(5))
def new_foo():
    raise RuntimeError("I'm always gonna fail")

try:
    new_foo()
except tenacity.RetryError as e:
    print("We caught the error: {}".format(e))
    # you would want to handle or re-raise here
```

The above will return something like:

> We caught the error: RetryError[<Future at 0x10702fa58 state=finished raised RuntimeError>]

It's likely this was a brain fart. If not I suggest looking into decorators. The short and sweet is that the above foo definition can be read as:

```
def new_foo():
    raise RuntimeError("I'm always gonna fail")

# wrap new_foo with tenacity retry and redefine new_foo as this wrapped fnc
new_foo = tenacity.retry(stop=tenacity.stop_after_attempt(5))(new_foo)
# The above hopefully makes it more clear that the retry object is what is raising RetryError and that new_foo never sees that exception.
```

@bosonogi I took a look at the code.

It looks like you're adding all events, with event timestamp and a json dump of the object.   Done that way, each instance of flower would need it's own database.  It should work fine for a persistence story, but doesn't seem a whole lot better than just file persist.

Another way to do it would be to expand the schema, and make an actual "events database" with separate tables for worker and task events.  With that approach, you could index different columns for lookup performance, and you could have multiple instances of flower "share" a database (e.g. don't duplicate an identical event record).  That would give you a reliability story if one flower instance was down, the other one would still be writing events.

this is sort of like the celery "snapshot" functionality, but writes could be done on the fly as events come in, rather than on a timer/tick.  Thoughts?

@johnarnold I have been delaying the inevitable by setting `FLOWER_MAX_TASKS` to 100000 and deleting tasks that are less relevant from the database to keep their total number smaller than that.

I didn't precisely measure startup time with event replay for these numbers, however it seems like it's on the order of 10-20 seconds.

An idea I contemplated and intended to explore is to make Flower stateless (or _pretty much_ stateless). I imagined doing this by having it rely on an external HTTP service to get task data using a well defined API. That way there could be different implementations of task data persistence independent of Flower, and it might make it possible to avoid the redundancy that currently exists for storing this data (celery task cache, flower's cache, result store, event store...). Also, this would make it easy to spin up multiple Flowers while having just a single task data storage service.

This sounds like a lot of work however, so I'll be thrilled if your approach turns out to be good enough :)

"deterministic" means that the augmentation process produces the same results if the input is the same AND the position in the batch is the same. Basically the RNG is reset at the *end* of the call to `augment_images()` or `augment_keypoints()`, but not *in between* images/keypoints. It is coincidence in your case that the third keypoint set is the same as the first one. Usually it would be different.

If you have several sets of keypoints and each one of them is supposed to be augmented in the identical way, you have to call `to_deterministic()` once and then `augment_keypoints([keypoints])` once per set of keypoints.

Hi, thanks for the question!

As you might know, thanks to the Berne Convention since 1886, a work doesn't need to be explicitly copyrighted for the copyright to be recognized. This means that even if it's not stated anywhere, the copyright belongs to the same people that would be listed in the notice.

So, rather than saying

> technically, nobody can follow the license terms

I'd rather describe the situation as

> nobody risks screwing up the license by misattributing or improperly modifying the copyright notice

I agree that the MIT license looks poorly worded, but you can think of the copyright notice that it's referring to as the "NULL", "0-length" copyright notice that is already included in the LICENSE file. In light of the above, adding a copyright to "pew contributors" is pretty much tautological and doesn't really add any useful information for anyone.

If you feel that you cannot fork/mirror/redistribute Pew due to the fact that you're afraid that it might be  violating the terms of the license, please take this current message as proof that you (or anyone else) are granted all the rights of the MIT license and can thus redistribute Pew without having to add a copyright notice.

(If you're creating a derivative work, there's nothing that says that you shouldn't adapt the copyright notice however your wish (while still maintaining the attribution correct) so, even if I'd prefer the notice to be omitted for the sake of brevity, there's nothing stopping you from adding it elsewhere)

One of the major goals of curio has been to remove the specifics of the underlying event loop from the picture (considering it to be a low-level implementation detail).   For example, you don't normally interact with the underlying Curio kernel in any way other than through coroutines using await.   In theory, it might be possible to swap in a different kernel implementation that would allow Curio applications to run on top of Twisted with few, if any, changes.  I've actually thought about trying this as a kind of API experiment, but have been a bit pressed for time lately.   So, it's not something I'm likely to look at right this moment.

My PyOhio talk has some further thinking about some of these ideas:  https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=E-1Y4kSsAFc

I agree. I think that alternate spellings included in the definitions file will not be a problem but they will have an overhead. There are to options when comparing a Unit to string

1.  Get the string representation of the unit and compare to the string.
2. Parse the string into a Unit

The first is fast but only handle very limited cases (individual units in canonical form, or compound units written in the same way that pint will write them). The second has an overhead (pint will need to parse the string) but will work in any situation. It is worth noting that for individual units or compound units already parsed the overhead will be only a dictionary lookup. So I think we should go for option number 2 by adding a specific case in Quantity.__eq__ and Unit.__eq__ for string comparisons.

@hugovk: I don't think you're describing the same issue as @franciscovargas.

Francisco commented that the `get_metadata('author', 2701)` call returned an *empty results set*.
You commented that the `get_metadata('author', 2701)` call returns a *set with a single entry*. If reproducible, the former is a bug; the later, however, is working as expected.

The `get_metadata` call returns a set because a book can have multiple authors, titles, etc, but it wouldn't make sense for the call to return the same piece of information twice; hence a set is the semantically appropriate data-structure.

If you know that the book for which you are retrieving meta-data such as authors only has a single author, using a helper function (like your `frozenhack`) to unpack the only element in the set makes sense. However, for the sake of consistency in the API, I assert that it should be the responsibility of clients of the library to do this unpacking, especially since it's as simple as `next(iter(get_metadata('author', 2701)))`.

Good question! What you describe is vignetting and this effect is part of the RAW image and caused by the physics of lenses. It would have to be corrected as part of some image postprocessing. libraw/dcraw does not do any lens corrections like distortion, chromatic abberation, or vignetting correction. There is, however, another library that deals with exactly that. It's called [lensfunpy](https://github.com/letmaik/lensfunpy) (which coincidently I'm the author of) that wraps [lensfun](http://lensfun.sourceforge.net/lenslist/). It looks like your camera/lens (Google Pixel) is not supported however. See http://lensfun.sourceforge.net/calibration/ on how to get your lens added to the database. Alternatively, you can take pictures with a camera/lens that's supported. There are probably also commercial softwares (e.g. from Adobe) that have support for more cameras/lenses.

This does sound useful @davidedelvento. Some control over how comparisons are made does seem in scope for a diff tool (e.g. whitespace sensitivity #36).

For development, the main step is to install `haxe`. Make sure you have version 3 or later.  The next step depends what language you are most comfortable in.  If you like javascript, make sure you have `node` installed, and then `make js` will compile daff for javascript and `make ntest_js` will run some tests.

Grepping `ignore_whitespace` in the source code will lead you to all the areas you might need to touch.  Basically three steps involved:
 * setting up and passing around a flag or flags
 * comparing floats for the diff (this is the core bit), see `TableDiff.isEqual`.
 * dealing with floats for indexing - roughly what should happen if a float is in primary key - you could skip this to start with.

Hmm, `db.__getitem__` is doing the sqlite query and raising FeatureNotFoundError when there are no results, and sqlite is pretty fast.

Then again, sqlite is disk-based. So I think it might be faster to do a one-time read of all existing exon IDs into a `set` object and check against that rather than the sqlite query. And to build that set, you might as well directly query rather than building Feature objects only to pull out their IDs:

```python
existing = set(
    i['id'] for i in db.execute(
        'select id from features where featuretype = "exon"')
)
```

You could also make an iterator of all novel exons, and pass that to `db.update` with additional kwarg `merge_strategy="warning"`. That effectively does the same thing as your function, so your code can be simplified. If you don't care which IDs you're getting collisions on, you could `import warnings` to filter them out.

You probably don't want `db.region`; it's doing a lot of extra work you don't need for this case.

Yup, the reason to do the AABB check on the point before the ray test is to exit higher in the pipeline. Also the rtree query implemented in ray_triangle is basically finding the AABB of the ray passing through the mesh volume and can have some pretty rough worst- case performance. If all the points are outside of the mesh's AABB we can avoid creating the rtree at all. 

Intersector objects in ray_triangle and ray_pyembree have identical interfaces, but the embree one is preferred if pyembree is installed. The one in ray_triangle is intended as a fallback and is somewhat naive, it does ~12,000 rays/s on my computer while embree was written by Intel and does roughly 650,000 rays/s on the same computer.

There isn't.

How do you group a continuous variable? You can't really, which means you have to bucket it. So on your side, you'd have to bucket age into logical groupings. E.g. 0-10, 10-20, 20-30 etc... and then make that a _ordinal_ categorical variable. You need a sufficient base size for each grouping, ideally.

Alternatively, if you had age ranges from 0-100 (the natural numbers up to 100), and tens of thousands of respondents, then you _could_ just leave it as is. However, with a smaller sample size, you'd have to create your groupings before hand, and then pass that in.

So I'm not really keen on committing to more low-level stuff because I don't have a full understanding of what's going on, but I found something that seems to work for me without causing troubles.

I noticed for some reason that quamash was getting the same Future instance twice and trying to set it as done twice... which [as the documentation notes](https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio-task.html#asyncio.Future.set_result), raises the InvalidStateError in question. To avoid this problem, I simply added a check to see if the future was already marked as complete [where the error occurs](https://github.com/harvimt/quamash/blob/master/quamash/_windows.py#L46).

```python
if not f.done():
    f.set_result(value)
```

Now I'm not trying to be dogmatic about this fix here, it just worked for me and I was trying something quick and easy. It might be a bigger problem that quamash is receiving and trying to mark the same Future as complete twice, or that ignoring the second send of the Future may end up being problematic. But hey, if you're looking for a quick fix in the meantime! Maybe someone more experienced can give better insight into the issue, though.

The first error is a common issue you get when a thread (the `Pool` uses threads internally for maintenance routine) is terminated abruptly by the interpreter. This is probably due to the fact you're not calling `Pool.join` which is highly recommended as, when not doing so, you might leak processes if your application keeps going.

I'd recommend you to call either `close` or `stop` and then `join` the `Pool`. The [documentation](pythonhosted.org/Pebble/) explain the difference between the methods.

The second issue is related but has a slightly different nature, it's due to one of the `worker` processes being terminated abruptly.

Both issues are harmless if triggering at shutdown. Still it's always better to call `join`.

I did not find country code information for ARIN's RDAP service (the other 3 RIR's provide it). Additionally, ARIN only provides address information un-parsed, in a inconsistent \n separated format, which differs from their restful API. I sent them an email, and here is their response:
> Thanks for your questions regarding RDAP.

> With regards to the country field, ARIN does not collect this information on networks. We only capture this information on organizations and points of contacts. Other RIRs do collect this information, and therefore they can populate that field in RDAP.

> The issue regarding addresses has a similar cause. RDAP uses jCard (vCard for JSON) to represent postal addresses. jCard has two forms of addresses, structured and unstructured. ARIN uses the unstructured form because ARIN does not collect address information in structured form completely (city, state, country, and postal code are collected in a structured manner, but the street address is not).

> I hope this answers your question, and thanks for your feedback.

I followed up with an email asking about parsing the country information from the address field, and if they have plans to provide a country code rather than the name (in all caps). I will update this thread when I get a response.

If ARIN parsing through RDAP is not an option, I may implement a standard parser for the other 3, and continue to use a custom parser to query ARIN's restful API.

More updates to come, including a standard result structure for RDAP.

Almost forgot: with the `keygen2=` argument to a column, you can pre-compute an ordering if you'd like, and explicitly create an index with that ordering.

```
def combine_fields(colx, coly):
    # say that we want to order by (colx, coly)
    # and we know -10000 < coly < 10000
    return colx * 10000 + coly

def compose_index(attr, dict):
        return {'': combine_fields(dict['colx'], dict['coly'])}

class IndexOnly(rom.Integer):
    _allowed = ()

class TestModel(rom.Model):
    custom_order = IndexOnly(index=True, keygen2=compose_index)
    ...

# used:
TestModel(colx=..., coly=...).save()

Oh, that's excellent! I had not taken a close enough look at `Constants` to discover the existing ability to configure this exactly how I described above.

So to wrap this up for anyone desiring to tweak this behavior, here is an example setup for parsing past and future dates relative to the current calendar year:
```python
import parsedatetime as pdt
con = pdt.Constants()

# When a year is not specified, assume the current year regardless
# of whether the date is before or after today
con.YearParseStyle = 0

# When given a day of week that is earlier than today 
# (e.g. *Monday* when today is Tuesday), assume that day 
# refers to the current week rather than the following week.
con.DOWParseStyle = 0

# When given a day of week matching today, parse as today's 
# date rather than one week later.
con.CurrentDOWParseStyle = 0

cal = pdt.Calendar(con)

cal.parse(...)
```
The source code has more complete documentation for those options; each supports `1` (the default), `0`, and `-1`. In general, use cases where future dates are entered will prefer the default option while other cases may prefer `0`. There are probably some specialized use cases for `-1` but that one is probably less common.

The way I see it there are 3 options

* i3-ipc and keybindings - This would allow us to listen to bound events. It is a bit messy but we could do something like `bindsym <key> nop py3status update volume_status` It adds a dependency of i3ipc python module.  It is also unavailable except via i3 config.

* dbus - This would add another python dependency but would allow `bindsym <key> py3status-command update volume_status` as well as running directly from the cli.

* sockets - This could be done without adding any dependencies and would have the similar usage as in the dbus example but we would need to specify a port and that starts making the whole thing more complicated.  `bindsym <key> py3status-command --port 1234 update volume_status` would be a pain although having a default port might help in most cases.

* some sort of shared file - This would have similar issues to sockets how do we choose which file to use.

The last 2 options also only work for a single instance of py3status and it is possible users may want more than one instance running in multiple bars.  So I think that the dbus approach is probably the usable one.  I have an old branch that adds this but it needs some work to make it compatible with the latest changes to py3status. To kick things off I put a "switch" class in the `172-simple-foreign-switch` branch sandbox.  The basic idea is that you would build an instance of each of the `BIPSimple` and `BIPForeign` classes, then wrap the two of them together into a "switch" and each of the instances are "terminals."  You start out with the first instance in the stack, then you can call `some_switch.switch_terminal(n)` the other instance is in the stack.  You could also provide a BBMD instance and switch to that.

Knowing when to switch would be up to your application, as well as kicking off the foreign device registration process when that became active.  We can add additional "activated" and "deactivated" methods if you want the terminal to know when it's becoming active, or add methods for the terminal to tell the switch to make it the active one, and maybe there shouldn't be an active terminal when it's created.

I'm not convinced that "switch" and "terminal" are very good names.

Building an APDU for `ReinitializeDeviceRequest` on the client side is about as simple as it gets, it only has one required parameter `reinitializedStateOfDevice` that is an enumeration:
```
    request = ReinitializeDeviceRequest(reinitializedStateOfDevice='warmstart')
```
If you want to support these services in your BACpypes application, define a `do_ReinitializeDeviceRequest(self, apdu)` function which will receive the request, send back an ack, and then comes the harder part.

You only need to support COLDSTART and WARMSTART.  For a cold start I've had some limited success spawning a subprocess `subprocess.run(['/sbin/reboot'])` but I try to stay away from it.  A warm start is easier because you can wrap the executable in a shell script and check for a special return code from your application, like `sys.exit(5)` which runs your application again.

I've also had good luck with a "supervisor" application that runs other programs on a scheduled basis like cron and restarts those that fail.  Almost everything that application can do is now available with `systemd` so I've been moving (slowly) in that direction.

> you provided:
>
> You're checking for replylimit.remaining once per iteration of the while
> loop and then replying to all mentions found in the outer loop within a
> subloop. What's probably happening is that you have, say, 1 reply remaining
> when you check for mentions in the outer loop. Then you get back, say, 5
> mentions in the api.GetMentions() call. You then enter the for loop
> attempting to reply to the 5 new mentions, but you've only got one allowed
> reply left, resulting in the rate limit error when you try to reply to the
> second mention (i.e., the 2nd iteration of the for loop)
>
> You could fix this by checking the reply limit within the for loop, but

Ok so as to why this is happening, if I'm not mistaken:

 * `HTMLRenderer.__init__` calls `BaseRenderer.__init__` with `(HTMLBlock, HTMLSpan)` as `extra`
 * `BaseRenderer.__init__` will then register `HTMLBlock` and `HTMLSpan` with e.g. `block_token.add_token`
 * so `HTMLBlock` and `HTMLSpan` will now be tokenized

And if the renderers are always called inside a context manager, it's all good since the `__exit__` takes care of the cleaning up.

I suggest updating the `README` to always use the `mistletoe.markdown` function, and never call the renderers directly. This may avoid future confusion.

Moreover, this means that the `ASTRenderer` and `LaTeXRenderer` do not comply with CommonMark since the do not register `HTMLBlock` and `HTMLSpan`. I guess it makes sense for `LaTeXRenderer`, not sure for `ASTRenderer`. Maybe add a note somewhere about that?

@niwinz thanks for the commit, I'll give it a shot when I get my laptop back. I do however prefer using extensions to make clear what the file is used for. The extension `.jinja` does not say anything about the file except that it is a Jinja2 template. Having extensions like `.txt` make it clear that the file is used for plaintext email templates for examples. `.html` is of course for HTML, `.scss` for SCSS files (these can still contain Jinja2 templates) and so forth. Using `.jijna` everywhere makes you lose a lot of context.

@mjtorn I am looking into this a bit, but I need input from @tholo.  I do not understand the purpose of the present options-parsing code which has several issues:

1. According to [the docs](http://flake8.pycqa.org/en/latest/user/configuration.html), configuring ignore options for `flake8` requires commas to separate the commands.  This `flake8-ignore` options however break if commas are used, thus one requires one of the following formats which are inconsistent:

   ```ini
   # contents of setup.cfg
   [tool:pytest]
   flake8-ignore = E201 E231

   [flake8]
   ignore = E201, E231
   ```
   or

   ```ini
   # contents of setup.cfg
   [tool:pytest]
   flake8-ignore = E201
                   E231

   [flake8]
   ignore = E201,
            E231
   ```
   (Note also that the configuration section has now been changed to `[tool:pytest]`)

Is there a use-case where one needs to control these separately?  If so, it would be good to at least use a consistent format.  If not, I would suspect that it is best to let `flake8` read its own configuration. From my reading of the source, `ignore` is the only option that was being clobbered -- all other should still work from the `flake8` configuration section.

Yes, this can be confusing, however I don't believe there is a general purpose solution.

The second error does actually make sense if you think about the heuristic Voluptuous uses when reporting errors for `Any()`: It attempts to match each clause separately and reports the *first, deepest error that failed*. In your example, because both matches fail at a depth of 1, it uses the first error it found, which is the mismatch for `field` in the first clause.

When you use `Any()` with potentially large common sub-trees, how do you pick which failure is the correct one? Perhaps it might be possible to track which subtree has the most success and report the error from that branch. This would require a fairly non-trivial amount of work though, as each validator would need to report its successful matches. I'm not 100% sure the effort is worth the gain.

https://github.com/facelessuser/backrefs/tree/real-format implements *true* string formatting.  Testing has been limited.  As mentioned, format strings don't really work in byte strings just like they don't work in Regex, but we we now get full access to all of the format strings features.  Interestingly, this implementation allows for format spec while Regex for some reason does not.  Haven't looked a how Regex implemented theirs.

Because the Regex match object doesn't make it easy to index properly (neither the capture object or match object return the last capture by default), we have to inject explicit indexes for sub-captures when they are implicit.  Because we are also handling each format group separately, we inject the main capture index as well. Afterwards we apply casing.

Like Regex, we are flexible with object indexes and allow for negative values, hex, octal, etc.  Though field names cannot be negative, octal, etc.

I am a little sad that Python does not support this format for byte strings.  I'd have to double check how it processes strings to be sure I can't just drop this in for byte strings.  I could construct a rudimentary byte string format, but I'm not sure if it is worth the effort to duplicate all these features for byte strings.  I could provide a more simplified method that doesn't provide all the format spec stuff...

> why is the namespacing necessary for this?

Well, in part, because this package is the one place that I'm aware of that supplies this functionality in a published package. If this functionality were published elsewhere, I'd happily deprecate the usage here and defer to someone else's contribution. I use namespace packages to publish my packages so that I don't spam PyPI with dozens of packages. I have 48 packages in GitHub and PyPI in the `jaraco`. I'm also a maintainer of several other packages that are in namespaces (`backports`, `svg`, `yg`, `keyrings`). Moreover, I drew this concept from experience in Java and working with other Python packages such as Zope. I think it would be disrespectful of me to lay claim to dozens of top-level names when one will suffice, especially for these modest packages.

It would be a crazy amount of work to deprecate the use of namespaces for what's implied herein - that namespace packages are essentially non-viable in popular packages.

I have more to say from the CherryPy perspective, so I'll say that in the PR.

I added a section on special rules `EMPTY` and `EOF` in the docs.

The problem with your parse is that your language is not LR(1). You can see that in state 10 there are two possible reductions for lookahead `NAMETK` and `eol`. LR parser can't parse that as it must deterministically decide at each step the right operation. 

parglare allows for R/R conflict as long as there is only one non-EMPTY reduction and in case of R/R it will prefer non-EMPTY reduction (this should probably be dropped and raised exception on any R/R conflict).

In this case this is not what you want. You must either change your grammar to fit LR(1) constraints or you can just switch to `Parser` -> `GLRParser` and let GLR investigate all possibilities.
I suggest to externalize the grammar in separate file and use `pglr` tool to check the grammar. It will report conflicts and can further visualize automata and GLR parsing trace.

3) (hostname not specified) - No strong feelings on my end about what's displayed.

5) (ssh -vv) - Thinking about it some more, this is a bigger scope than `-vv`. What I'm really looking for is `assh wrapper ssh` to look and act as much like `ssh` as possible, since the generated `~/.ssh/config` isn't usable by `ssh`. That's a harder problem to solve.

6) (assh connect --dry-run) - My expectation is that this command will succeed if my config is valid, and show me what the connection will look like. When it failed, I assumed that meant I had a problem with that host. I think your answer to 7) is what I really need. :-)

8) (ssh aliases) - I've realized there's another issue here.

Example ssh config:

    Host foo*.bar
        IdentityFile ~/ssh/special

    Host *.bar
        User me
        IdentitifyFile ~/ssh/regular

ssh uses the first value found for each setting. This config will use the `regular` key for all *.bar servers but use the `special` key for all foo*.bar servers. But if I create the same config in assh:

    hosts:
        "foo*.bar":
            identityfile: ~/ssh/special
        "*.bar":
            identityfile: ~/ssh/regular
            user: me

This generates the following config file:

    Host *.bar
        IdentityFile ~/ssh/regular
        User me

    Host foo*.bar
        IdentityFile ~/ssh/special

The ordering has been reversed! This will use the wrong key file for foo servers.

Performance of A Jetson TX2 in single-precision floating point is about 1.5 TFLOPS, which is relatively small compared to common PC GPUs such as GTX970(4.2TFLOPS). And its memory bandwidth is less than 1/3 of GTX970. (see discussions on CUDA forum https://devtalk.nvidia.com/default/topic/1024825/jetson-tx2-performance/).
Doing a face_encodings on an 1080p image takes about 1.5-2 secs with 970. So your device could use 3x time to complete the same task, not to mention you may have set a different upsample parameter which would greatly increase task scale and memory consumption.

Just need some modifications for your code.
Take the **Factory Example** in README

```python
# extensions.py
from flask_celery import Celery

celery = Celery()
```

```python
# application.py
from flask import Flask
from extensions import celery

def create_app():
    app = Flask(__name__)
    app.config['CELERY_IMPORTS'] = ('tasks.add_together', )
    app.config['CELERY_BROKER_URL'] = 'redis://localhost'
    app.config['CELERY_RESULT_BACKEND'] = 'redis://localhost'
    celery.init_app(app)

    # Make configured Celery instance attach to Flask
    app.celery = celery

    return app
```

```python
# tasks.py
from extensions import celery

@celery.task()
def add_together(a, b):
    return a + b
```

```python
# manage.py
from application import create_app

app = create_app()

# Use `celery -A manage.celery worker` to start celery worker
celery = app.celery

app.run()
```

Hello,

The `delete_translations` is the right one, it delete all translations matching the query, so all you have to do is filter them first. Here is how you would do it:
```python
# Delete a single translation
MyModel.objects.language('de').filter(pk=42).delete_translations()
# or, if you already have the object loaded
MyModel.objects.language('de').filter(pk=instance.pk).delete_translations()

# Delete several translations at once
MyModel.objects.language('all').filter(pk=42, language_code__in=['de', 'fr', 'en']).delete_translations()

# An alternate way that also works
instance.translations.filter(language_code__in=['de', 'fr']).delete()
```
As always with Django, the affected translations will be deleted from the database but remain in your loaded instance until it goes out of scope.

As a sidenote, it is your responsibility to ensure you do not delete all translations: there must always be at least one translation for an object, otherwise hvad will raise warnings.

That's an interesting suggestion. I could integrate have `emit` call `wait(seconds=0.1)` and it might simplify code that uses this library.

On second thought, I think I should leave it up to the user to decide when to process events. In the current implementation, the `emit` method sends the packets to the server right away but does not process any responses from the server until the `wait` method is called.

I think I separated the `wait` method in order to let the user control when to process events. For example, the user might want to send a bunch of `emits` first before processing any events. This is especially important if some of the events involve callbacks to the server.

By forcing the user to process events from the server separately in a `wait`, we let the user control the sequence of initial emits in a reliable way. Subsequently calling `wait` activates the event-based portion, where timing and order can vary depending on the network.

```class PublishManager(models.Manager):

    get_queryset = get_query_set

Hi, thanks for the detailed explanation. While illegal memory accesses could be indicating a bug in our implementation (we'd have to run with cuda memcheck to verify), out of memory errors should be straight forward and it should be no problem to reduce the tensor sizes. I just want to make you aware of the fact that we got 8GB of GPU memory available and that we don't run GPU tests in parallel. In case you'd like to reduce the sizes, please consider whether the tensors are actually too big or if there could be other problems in our implementation.

I'm not 100% familiar with the details, so please bare with me if my questions don't make sense. In general, I understand that you have some special optimized features in mkldnn which are triggered by certain tensor sizes and memory layouts. My question here is how the user is able to influence that: are they able to select a certain layout or is this something we do implicitly in the backend? If they are able to select it, but it's not available in the current backend (because it's not supported), how does the behaviour continue? Is there a fallback mechanism? What we should avoid is to add features that are only usable in very certain environments. In general, we would like our users to define their networks without the backend in mind and trust us that we are doing our best to execute it most effectively - or at least fall back to a method which will still deliver correct results but may be slower (we've been thinking about an Auto Tuning mechanism for the future to select the best implementation). Please understand that we want to avoid having a vendor lock in our models - users should not be restricted to MKLDNN, cuda oder cudnn if they design their networks.

If everything you'd like to test is completely happening in the background, you're free to make mkldnn specific tests. But in that case, I'd assume that they'd be cpp tests, considering the assumption that this does not depend on the frontend and thus would be ineligible for a Python test.

I'm making a lot of assumptions here, so please don't hesitate to correct me if something does not match the reality.

```
class PReLU(gluon.HybridBlock):
    def __init__(self, *args):
        super(PReLU, self).__init__(*args)
        with self.name_scope():
            self.alpha = self.params.get('alpha', shape=(1,), init=mx.init.Zero())
            self.act = gluon.nn.Activation(activation='relu')

    def hybrid_forward(self, F, x, alpha):
        pos = self.act(x)
        neg = F.negative(alpha) * self.act(F.negative(x))
        return pos + neg
```

This is how hybrid block works

If your columns are timezone aware it doesn't matter what timezone the server uses. That is why we are using timezone aware columns.

Changing this potentially causes exactly the problem you are trying to avoid: suppose I am in -7:00 timezone and my server is set to UTC; now I create a new timezone aware datetime at 15:00-7:00 but I calculate now using utcnow, what ends up being created is 24:00-7:00, which gets stored as 7:00UTC the next day.

If columns are timezone aware, feed them timezone aware, then the columns and datetime objects will take care of all of this for you.

This is NOT a bug; this is working as intended.

Reasonably, one might want to use `server_default=func.now()` so that the server does the calculations and we don't worry about network latency but that's a different issue

The default configuration for watchman is to have it try to aggregate watches at a git, mercurial or svn repo.  If you have a repo in your home directory (some folks do this to keep track of their dotfiles) then this may be what is happening.

If you don't want your home dir to be watched, then you have a couple of options.

1.  Place a `.watchmanconfig` file at the root of what you consider to be the project for your React Native or Nuclide usage.  This will short-circuit the walk that finds your home dir and stop at the dir that contains the `.watchmanconfig` file.  This should work fine provided that those tools are not explicitly trying to operate on your home dir.

2. Create a file named `/etc/watchman.json` with the contents:

```
{
   "root_files": [".watchmanconfig"],
   "enforce_root_files": true
}
```

Some background about this, it's basically Webpack creating an AMD loader that doesn't completely quack like one:
https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/4465

It seems one option is to fully disable AMD in Webpack if you don't need it, which will let Migrate follow the CommonJS path and then it works. See this ticket:
https://github.com/webpack/webpack/issues/3017#issuecomment-285954512

Now the question is, does it make sense to revert AMD support, publish 3.0.2, then re-commit AMD support and publish a 3.1.0 that will *still* break anyone using `~` or `>` in their package.json? Or is it sufficient for people to see the solution in this ticket?
The second answer on that question is more correct. Might and may can be used interchangeable in the present tense, so this PR isn't necessary.

I'm not necessarily opposed, but some of the statements here are inaccurate:

> Importing in function is bad practice

I'm not necessarily opposed to the change, but this statement is untrue. Delaying rarely used and/or costly imports until they are going to be used is a common and sensible optimization in Python. That said, these particular rarely-used imports probably aren't costly enough to be worth delaying, so I think it's probably sensible to put them back to simple top-level imports.

The real source of the problem here, though, is triggering the launch of threads on import. That's very strongly recommended against, and the cause of the deadlock. It is absolutely not a valid assumption in Python that calling a function cannot trigger imports. There are *lots* of ways for nontrivial logic, especially with threads, during imports to cause problems and hangs. The first thing to do is to move the thread-launching code in your `file1.py` module into a function so that it doesn't get instantiated on import. This is the right thing to do regardless of whether the pyzmq imports change. If your module is also a script, this is the reason the `if __name__ == '__main__':` idiom exists.

this does not work with gpg either, fwiw
```bash
$ echo foo > bar
$ cp bar baz
$ gpg -e bar
$ gpg -e baz
$ sha256sum bar.gpg baz.gpg 
df9a2e827d5d6946d40c1c7b25c7bdbfa8f78102f259ebff2b7351d48247b73f  bar.gpg
b0aa36688bd193aa70cefe7019ee4e368390ea7370269dfb0a85eb358c96fc00  baz.gpg
```
the reason for this is encryption tools generate random data encryption keys for each file, and then encrypt the data key with the private key (for pgp) or kms (for sops kms). Running sops twice with create 2 different data keys that will result in two different files, regardless of what the IV values are.

You should modify your secret.yaml file using sops as an editor instead of recreating the file every time from scratch.

```bash
$ sops -p MY_KEY -e secret.yaml > encrypted.yaml
$ git add encrypted.yaml, etc...
```
then when you want to modify encrypted.yaml, do

`$ sops encrypted.yaml`

make changes and git commit, it will show the right diff

For simplicity we only track references to objects that can't be ordinarily encoded by json as-is. We don't track dicts or lists but I can see how that could be useful, and it is a better, more general solution.

The referencing machinery is something that could benefit from a redesign.  Right now the IDs stored in json are basically the index into the list of objects we've seen as traversing, which can be fragile and probably would break down if we were to properly handle list and dict references.

A better way might be to change the storage into a flat object dict keyed by IDs (`id(...)` like we currently do?) that we store and restore in dependency (and reverse) order.  In order to ensure that we can recreate all references we could also store the list of keys so that the decoder can traverse the objects in the reverse order as it did when encoding them.

If you `git grep _Proxy` you'll see one part of the current design that would be really great to eliminate/redo.  Another possibly questionable thing is that we're dependent on the final json being written with sorted keys (`git grep sort`).  It would be nice if that wasn't required.

@mslinn I think I found a possible solution (at least for Python 3). Looking at the objects in boto3. I had a similar problem using pickle with a particularly large object. Here's what I got working:
```
>>> obj
s3.Object(bucket_name='my-bucket', key='path/to/data/pickled-object.pkl')
>>> body = obj.get()["Body"]
>>> body
<botocore.response.StreamingBody object at 0x10e48d978>
>>> body._raw_stream
<botocore.vendored.requests.packages.urllib3.response.HTTPResponse object at 0x10e48d3c8>
```

So looking at the docs for this response here http://urllib3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/#module-urllib3.response you can see:
>class urllib3.response.HTTPResponse(body='', headers=None, status=0, version=0, reason=None, strict=0, preload_content=True, decode_content=True, original_response=None, pool=None, connection=None, retries=None, enforce_content_length=False, request_method=None)
> Bases: io.IOBase

This is good news because Python offers some nice IO objects to allow for BufferedIOReading (https://docs.python.org/3/library/io.html#io.BufferedReader). So continuing from the code above:

```
>>> import io
>>> buff_reader = io.BufferedReader(body._raw_stream)
>>> buff_reader
<_io.BufferedReader>
>>> import pickle

Raw binary will only work in python3, you appear to be using python 2 so you must wrap your binary data in the special `boto3.dynamodb.types.Binary` type detailed [here](http://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/customizations/dynamodb.html#ref-valid-dynamodb-types).

Below is a working code segment for both py2 and py3:

```python
import boto3
import uuid
from Crypto.Cipher import AES
from  boto3.dynamodb.types import Binary

boto3.set_stream_logger('')

table = 'test-table'
message = 'this is super seecrit'
salt = 'this is the salt'
iv = 'this is the iv23'

obj = AES.new(salt, AES.MODE_CFB, iv)
crypto_text = obj.encrypt(message)
print(crypto_text, type(crypto_text))


item = {
    'uuid': str(uuid.uuid4()),
    'cryptotext': Binary(crypto_text)
}

dynamodb = boto3.resource('dynamodb', region_name='us-east-1')
table = dynamodb.Table(table)
table.put_item(Item=item)

```

Hope that helps!

As I said in the other issue:

> I'm not implementing that in fuzzywuzzy, but you are free to do so in a wrapper function by just checking if one string is in another. Note that you'll definitely incur a performance penalty.

Again, I don't think you can relicense your fork as MIT. MIT => GPLv2 is okay, but not in the reverse.

[Here is an issue](https://github.com/seatgeek/fuzzywuzzy/issues/130) where we discussed trying to include _just_ the individual license on the StringMatcher library.

Even if it were possible for me to relicense it, there are a couple issues here:

- You are not the copyright holder of fuzzywuzzy, SeatGeek is. According to the GPLv2, you cannot relicense your fork. If you made a clean-room implementation, its potentially possible, but see [Google v Oracle](https://www.theinquirer.net/inquirer/news/3029302/a-google-vs-oracle-copyright-ruling-could-well-kill-small-scale-open-source-projects) for a ruling where we might be out of luck.
- We cannot relicense from GPLv2 to MIT without getting express permission from everyone who has contributed for the lifetime of the project. Twitter Bootstrap [did this](https://github.com/twbs/bootstrap/issues/2054) and it took forever. We could probably do this, but maintaining this library from an OSS perspective isn't strictly part of my work responsibilities, and we don't have the resources to do so.

My understanding here is that only SeatGeek - as the copyright holder - can relicense the project from GPLv2 to MIT, and only once we stop using python-Levenshtein completely, and then _only with_ express permission of _all_ those who have contributed to the project. Others are free to fork our project, but GPLv2 requires that they keep the same license or use a compatible license (and only for new changes).

[Copyleft licenses (GPLv2) are not compatible with Permissive licenses (MIT)](https://blog.gerv.net/2013/02/relicensing-when-do-you-have-to-ask/), meaning no one is currently allowed to relicense fuzzywuzzy or derivatives to MIT.

> Note: I am not a lawyer, I am merely referencing other reading online.

Thanks @eliasdorneles! I think the current pull request is fine, and the only unresolved question is whether attrib should be None or an empty dict for empty SelectorList objects. 

@dangra wrote:

> Keeping the discussion around .attrib property, I think the only thing that worries me is the last point about caller not being able to distinguish between non-matching node and node without attributes.

So, let's check example use patterns:

```py
# --- get an attribute, raise an error if attribute is not present:
sel.css('a').attrib['href']  # in both cases

# --- get an attribute, use None as its value, but raise an error if selector is empty:

# None
sel.css('a').attrib.get('href')

# {}, though not exactly equivalent
sel.css('a')[0].attrib.get('href')

# --- get an attribute, use None as its value when attribute 
# is not present or a selector is empty:

# None
(sel.css('a').attrib or {}).get('href')

# {}
sel.css('a').get('href')
```

Examples convinced me None is better: it allows to distinguish between non-matching selector and a first element without attributes, and is not much harder to use when the opposite behavior is required. It also looks easier to debug in cases you've got a selector wrong, there will be less questions about "element has attributes, but css('my-selector").attrib is empty".

So +1 to switch to None, add an example to parsel docs, and merge.

Thanks @saran87. I dug deep into this and learned a few things. Consider this code:
```python
from threading import Thread
from multiprocessing import Process
import os

class MyThread(Thread):                                                                                                                                                      
    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        self.parent = current_thread()
        Thread.__init__(self, *args, **kwargs)

def run_thread():                                                                                                                                                                     
    print "in thread: {}".format(current_thread())
    print "in thread pid: {}".format(os.getpid())

def start_thread():
    t = MyThread(target=run_thread)
    t.start()
    print "parent: {}".format(t.parent)
    print "process thread: {}".format(current_thread())
    print "subprocess id: {}".format(os.getpid())
    t.join()

print "main process: {}".format(os.getpid())
p = Process(target=start_thread)                                                                                                                                                      
p.start()                                                                                                                                                                             
```
The output from this code surprised me:
```
main process: 4798
in thread: <MyThread(Thread-1, started 139802621290240)>
in thread pid: 5179
parent: <_MainThread(MainThread, started 139802680162048)>
subprocess id: 5179
process thread: <_MainThread(MainThread, started 139802680162048)>
```
This shows that the thread running on the child process being created is actually the same `Thread` instance as the main interpreter thread. This seems to imply that communicating between the main thread in which you've created your consumer instance and its worker threads amounts to interprocess communication. Specifically, communication between the `MyThread` instance and its parent thread (`_MainThread`) is also communication between process `5179` and process `4798`, since the latter is the original owner of `_MainThread`.

I'm not certain how sharing the main thread between the main process and a subprocess works (ie whether my theory above is valid), but the evidence seems to support it. My test code is essentially a simplified recreation of what you're doing in your code - spawning a child process, then running a consumer that makes its own threads. Since the consumer internally uses `Queue.Queue` to pass data between the main thread and child threads, it's not able to pass data between different processes (that's what `multiprocessing.Queue` is for). Attempts to implement a `ProcessHandler` that uses `multiprocessing.Queue` soon result in errors related to pickling.

As far as I can tell, PyKafka doesn't support subprocess execution for this reason. The current options are to use `threading.Thread` or to check out #368's gevent support.

Pystorm (a project Streamparse uses to implement components in Python), has an attempt to fix the stdout print problem, though it should have been initialized by the time the `process` function ran. https://github.com/pystorm/pystorm/blob/3c91e04675b3d2a2d1facb6c19908b82b018086a/pystorm/component.py#L279-L282. What version of Python did you attempt to run on?

In general we recommend you use the `self.logger` object provided by the spouts and bolts, as seen here: https://github.com/Parsely/streamparse/blob/master/examples/redis/src/bolts.py#L23.

I'm generally against such source layouts for a couple reasons:
1) It's a bit more of a pain to do in-source imports.  One has to add `src/` to Python-path somehow, or explicitly use `./setup.py develop`.  It's nicer to just be able to cd to the source directory and import from there.
2) A lot of Python-based tools (like astropy-helpers, apparently!) just don't support that very well.

All that said, there are some packages where 1) doesn't necessarily apply--especially projects that are not pure-Python.  And also I'm not one to force a particular source layout on anyone either, so if this change made that work and you've tested it I'm all for it.  (In fact a test would be kind of nice to have for this, but I won't demand it--it's hard to write such a test, especially such that it hits all the relevant code).

Hi Pengyu

In almost all instances, there should only be 1 launch in that field. The
exception to this is rare and involves duplicate checking with past jobs -
i.e., a current Firework might have duplicate matched to several historical
launches from different Fireworks that were all completed sucessfully, all
of which are considered to be valid launches of the current Firework. The
reason that the key is called "launches" is because in such situations
there could indeed be multiple valid launches for a Firework, and
historically in the first use case of Fireworks, this was a more major
concern and needed to be handled. For most users, I think it is OK to think
of launches as only have a single launch.

Best,
Anubhav

any ideas?

- debugging : it's native code, so you ``just'' need to run ``gdb --arg python my_python_script_that_calls_a_native_lib.py`` and set your breakpoint etc.

- profiling : there is no such tool as ``cython -a``, which makes sense because everything is pure C++, no Python C-API. So the basic tool like ``callgrind`` apply. There is still a ``pythran -P`` command that lets you observe the transformed file *before* it gets dump to C++ and ``pythran -E`` that generates the .cpp file for inspection, tracing etc.

- fused types are supported, in the sense that

  ```python
  #pythran export foo(int[])
  #pythran export foo(float[])
  def foo(x): return x
   ```
   works as expected.

- advantages over cython: cleanr syntax, and thiner binaries (see http://serge-sans-paille.github.io/pythran-stories/shrinking-pythran-generated-binaries.html)

- disadvtanges over cython : longer compilation time, no mixed mode (if a function / package is not supported, you need to implement it), no support for user classes

@jni I dont have a concrete proof that this will always result in the best factorization but here it goes. We need `4r ≈ 3c ≈ k` which implies `r ≈ k/4` and `c ≈ k/3`. Let `Total images = N`. Now `rc = N`, which implies `k^2 = 12*N`, which gives `k =  (12*N)**0.5`. Now we get `r = int(k/4)` or `r = int(k/4) + 1`, and get `c = N/r` or `c = N/r + 1`. And just check which of the two gives a closer ratio of `r:c = 3:4`

A few examples: 

- N = 20, k = 15.49, r = 3 or 4, c = 7 or 5, and we choose r = 4 , c = 5, giving 4r = 16, 3c = 15
- N = 33, k = 19.87, r = 4 or 5, c = 9 or 7, and we choose r = 5, c = 7, giving 4r = 20, 3c = 21
- N = 43, k = 22.71, r = 5 or 6, c = 9 or 8, and we choose r = 6, c = 8, giving 4r = 24, 3c = 24

Do correct me if I made some error.

Regarding the progressbar2, have you looked at _needs_update() method? https://github.com/WoLpH/python-progressbar/blob/develop/progressbar/bar.py#L407  I'm really interested why it does't work for you. It should perform approximately as many updates as there are horizontal characters in your terminal, so there is no point to divide the value by 1000. When you call `update()` in most cases the code path should be quite short, from line https://github.com/WoLpH/python-progressbar/blob/develop/progressbar/bar.py#L429 to line https://github.com/WoLpH/python-progressbar/blob/develop/progressbar/bar.py#L451. The overhead of this short path can be still noticeable if you call `update()` a lot, but then it is much better to disable progressbar completely (`--disable-progressbar` or something like this).

I did a benchmark and on my machine clean import without `update_interval` patch took 22:22, with `update_interval` patch it took 20:06 (I haven't disabled `reset_queries()` and `_travis()` to test the effect in isolation).

So in my history of working with transactional databases I've run into and / or wanted to write lots of code in which certain functions may or may not start transactions, allowing them to be reused by other functions that also may or may not want to start transactions. It seemed somewhat common but I've lived for many years now with the transaction package not supporting it. Obviously it's a code smell to have poorly defined division of responsibilities.

A more practical example is coming from something like `pyramid_tm` where if it's in the pipeline it's going to manage the transaction for you. We've run into countless cases in pyramid where people do database operations before or after pyramid_tm gets control of the transaction. This starts an implicit transaction right now and the user is unaware that they're doing anything wrong. The request then hits pyramid_tm which (silently) closes their transaction and starts a new one invalidating whatever work they had done. We'd like to handle this error-prone case in a better way. At the very least I'd say that raising an exception when calling `begin()` twice in a row without an `abort()`/`commit()` would be good but ideally you could also detect the already-open transaction and start using it, turning the pyramid_tm manager into a no-op.

On PyPy, WeakKeyDictionary isn't cleaned up immediately when objects die, so iterating it and listing its keys and values can return stale entries or even contradict each other:
```pycon
Python 2.7.13 (84a2f3e6a7f8, Oct 03 2017, 18:46:00)
[PyPy 5.9.0 with GCC 4.2.1 Compatible Apple LLVM 5.1 (clang-503.0.40)] on darwin
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>>> class Foo(object):
....     def __hash__(self):
....         return 42
....    def __eq__(self, other):
....       return isinstance(other, Foo)
....
>>>> from weakref import WeakKeyDictionary
>>>> d = WeakKeyDictionary()
>>>> d[Foo()] = 1
>>>> d[d.keys()[0]]
1
>>>> d.keys()
[<__main__.Foo object at 0x00000001106fe090>]
>>>> d = WeakKeyDictionary()
>>>> d[Foo()] = 1
>>>> d.items()
[]
>>>> d[Foo()] = 1
>>>> d.values() # Contradiction!
[1, 1]
>>>> import gc
>>>> gc.collect()
0
>>>> d.values()
[]
```

(Compare to CPython:
```pycon
Python 2.7.14 (default, Sep 27 2017, 12:15:00)
[GCC 4.2.1 Compatible Apple LLVM 9.0.0 (clang-900.0.37)] on darwin
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> class Foo(object):
...   def __hash__(self):
...     return 42
...   def __eq__(self, other):
...     return isinstance(other, Foo)
...
>>> from weakref import WeakKeyDictionary
>>> d = WeakKeyDictionary()
>>> d[Foo()] = 1
>>> d.keys()
[]
```
)

*Fortunately*, the weak refs themselves that are used internally *are* guaranteed not to compare equal, even if they hash equal, when one of them is dead, so the trivial exercise of looking one up exactly always does work:
```pycon
>>>> d = WeakKeyDictionary()
>>>> class Foo(object):
....    def __hash__(self):
....        return 42
....    def __eq__(self, other):
....       return isinstance(other, Foo)
....
>>>> d[Foo()] = 42
>>>> d[Foo()]
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "//pypy/lib-python/2.7/weakref.py", line 356, in __getitem__
    return self.data[ref(key)]
KeyError: <weakref at 0x00000001106e3720; to 'Foo'>
```

But if you do anything other than that, watch out.

       from rdflib import Graph, URIRef

so this calls `bind` with the namespace URL: `http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/GENO_`

this gets put in the cache table.

Update: there's something else going on here that I'm investigating today. It doesn't just have to do with the DOM size, because the region of the DOM that `searchPseudoElements()` searches through is relatively small in the case of the original repro offered by @transbetacism (which I _was_ able to use to reproduce the problem on my dev machine). When the Customizer is open, it's putting the normal page inside a frame, and then it's just a subset of what's in that frame that is being searched for pseudo elements. So the relevant DOM size is not the "Logged in with Customizer Open" number, actually. It's—at most—one of those smaller numbers.

Rather, I've discovered what appears to be a feedback loop that occurs when the Customizer is open, where huge amounts of spurious DOM updates are being generated between the mutation observer and the pseudo element replacement code. But I haven't quite got to the bottom of it yet.

Stay tuned...

Hi guys,

(A VTE developer here...)

Thanks a lot to both of you for working on this!

Guake is a truly popular terminal emulator. Whether it's the actual terminal emulation behavior (VTE) or the UI around it that is more important for most people is hard to tell. Probably some combination of the two.

VTE has received tons and tons of fixes and improvements during the last couple of years. There is a point, maybe not yet at 90% but maybe at 95% or 98% or who knows, where you should probably say the improvements offered by new VTE surely outweigh the few regressions in new Guake. It's your choice, I'd only like to ask you not to aim for 100% as it'll probably never be reached.

You keep mentioning here and in README.rst that you've ported to VTE library version 2.90 (which corresponds to tarball versions up to 0.36). Luckily, by looking at the source it seems that you actually ported to 2.91 (tarballs from 0.38). This was an incompabitle change 3 years ago. The 2.90 branch, just as the gtk2 one, is no longer maintained. Furthermore, most distributions have already dropped the "middle" (2.90) version of the package, whereas they're yet to get rid of the gtk2 one.

Looking forward to a stable gtk3 release obsoleting the gtk2 one as soon as possible :-)

Feel free to ask me if you have questions related to VTE.

PS. According to my experiences, complete rewrites hardly ever work. Tons of incremental changes, trying to keep the stuff as stable as reasonable, is the way to go.

Pour le test en question peut-être que _Cas N°1_ pourrait être remplacé par :
- tranche 3/3 (ie. ressources élevées) ou bien
- 3ème tranche.

- [ ] Supprimer les tests ayant un `ignore`.
- [ ] Retirer le support pour `ignore`.
- [ ] Retirer le support pour le préfixe `IGNORE_` et commenter les lignes correspondantes.
- [ ] Systématiquement exécuter tous les tests en CI.

Si le besoin apparaît de créer des "spécifications", c'est-à-dire si des contributeurs souhaitent contribuer des tests sans être capables de contribuer leur implémentation :

1. Passer par des branches _long-lived_. Le coût de mise à jour sera ainsi payé en une seule fois quand un contributeur capable de coder les formules nécessaires sera disponible.
2. Alternative : reprendre le concept de _ignore_, avec un attribut _spec_ ou similaire. Mais cette fois, les tests sont systématiquement exécutés et un résultat d'échec est attendu, et la CI échoue si une erreur est levée (garantit la mise à jour de ces tests) ou si les tests passent (augmente la couverture des effets de bord).

Okay, so in ODIM H5 files when a sweep's azimuth angles are not explicitly given in the `startazA` and `stopazA` attributes then as per section 5.1 of the standards doc (version 2.2) the polar data should be ordered starting at 0 degrees and proceed clockwise.  The **index** of the first ray collected in time is given by the `a1gate` attribute in the dataset's where group.  I had misread the meaning of the `a1gate` attribute, using it as the first azimuth angle, which is leading to the rotation issue. > .then grpc will send results into the queue whenever a change happens

No. The `queue` is owned by the caller to send requests. The **only** place `ListenResponse`-s get sent is into the `response_gen` iterator (it is an instance of the `_Rendezvous` god-class).

@vrcs The back-end [`extract` operation](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/reference/v2/jobs#configuration.extract) doesn't support such an option.  To achieve this goal, you could:

- Run an asynchronous `QueryJob`, passing a temporary table as its `destination`.
- Run an `ExtractJob` on that temporary table.

Untested sketch:

```python
import time
from google.cloud.bigquery import Client

client = Client()
dataset = client('my_dataset')
temp_table = dataset.table('query-results')
assert not temp_table.exists()
query = 'SELECT * from my_dataset.widgets WHERE frob_count >= 100')

query_job = client.run_async_query('query-frobbed-widgets', query)
query_job.destination = temp_table
query_job.begin()

while query_job.state.lower() != 'done':
    query.job.reload()
    time.sleep(1)

extract_job = client.extract_table_to_storage(
    'extract-query-results', temp_table,
    'gs://my-bucket-name/frobbed-widgets*')
extract_job.destination_format = 'CSV'
extract_job.begin()

while extract_job.state.lower() != 'done':
    extract.job.reload()
    time.sleep(1)
```

Please feel free to re-open the issue if that example doesn't help.

So I did some more digging and it turns out the `Reference` protobuf in App Engine is `proto1` (i.e. first generation of protobuf), so the modern `proto2/proto3` tooling can't convert it. I got [very close][1] to converting using standard tools, but some small differences in the protocols prevent it.

You can **definitely** include the `ProtocolBuffer` module in your application (in any location):

```
google-cloud-sdk/platform/google_appengine/google/net/proto/ProtocolBuffer.py
```

and then just define your own local `Reference` and `Path` classes from

```
google-cloud-sdk/platform/google_appengine/google/appengine/datastore/entity_pb.py
```

I.e. you can use these files outside of App Engine. I have done so in the gist I linked to and it works just fine.

[1]: https://gist.github.com/dhermes/9d658dc68c55f042f9766490721ac16a

    def __init__(self, key_file, cert_file):
        urllib.request.HTTPSHandler.__init__(self)
        self.key_file = key_file
        self.cert_file= cert_file

    def https_open(self, req):
        return self.do_open(self.getConnection, req)

Hi Nicklas,
The short answer is NO.
You can't issue commands to Sonos from the browser.

You can read information from Sonos by using port 1400 eg: http://[Sonos
player IP]:1400/status
more info at: https://github.com/SoCo/SoCo/wiki/Information-Adresses

To get Sonos to do something you need to send it a upnp command.
You can do this from a Python program using SoCo,.

There is also a command line interface called SoCo/Socos see:
https://github.com/SoCo/socos
This allows you to enter lines in Dos or Linux to make Sonos do things

There are also utilities such as Device Spy that allow you to send UNP
commands.

Hope this helps.
Cheers David

```
from __future__ import print_function
try:
    from queue import Empty
except:  # Py2.7

import soco
from pprint import pprint
from soco.events import event_listener

device = soco.SoCo('192.168.1.71')
print (device.player_name)  # prove we have a valid zone for testing

sub_av = device.avTransport.subscribe()

while True:
    try:
        event = sub_av.events.get(timeout=0.5)
        pprint(event.variables)
    except Empty:
        pass

    except KeyboardInterrupt:  # (control C on windows)
        sub_av.unsubscribe()
        event_listener.stop()
        break

This isn't easy:

 * The SQLite online backup API is implemented in C and I haven't found Python bindings (well compiled ones, but that won't play)
 * The `sqlite3` CLI does implement this as `.backup` however the CLI isn't necessarily on the target system just because the library is!
 * `django-dbbackup` uses a very different approach of creating a dump that gets restored with a special restore command, so it's not just the `.sqlite` file that can be copied in-place.

I'm going for an approach where somehow we can do a simple, naive file copy that will work for most people, supposing that no other kolibri is running during the upgrade (which we should have some other mechanisms to guarantee!)

I'll defer the style of file i/o to your, @ibnesayeed . One approach requires we only have a single file handler but keeps it open through a potentially long process whereas the second has two short-lived handlers but has other issues of other data being written to the target file if written from another process in the meantime.

With that in-mind, I think I prefer the first version but will still defer.

> This would allow a subset of the CDXJ entries to be locally pinned instead of requiring everything listed in the index.

You are mixing something up here. At the moment, losing entries from the CDX would be disastrous, even if those entries are for the embedded resources, because from the replay perspective, they are all independent resources, it is the browser that put them together to compose the page they way it looks. Pinning resources locally and losing entries from the CDX would make them non-discoverable. Pinning, in my opinion can be done separately as a batch process independent of the replay system. One can grab the list of hashes extracted from the index or from the access log (or from any other source for that matter) and drain them down locally and pin them. This should be done at the node where the IPFS is running, and not necessarily couple with the replay. I think, separation of concerns is very important here.

Thanks for the note. Pymatgen supports both py27 and Py3k, which is why some Py3 functions are duplicated in monty. Essentially, these are backports of Py3k functionality. When Py3k becomes the only Py version in wide use, we will remove some of these monty features.

However, I think the functionality that you mention is different. 

1. The archiving operations of shutil creates tar archives. This is not always what you want (and not what we wanted for the specific use case in custodian where we gzip all the results of the run but wants to retain the ability to look into the files using zless). monty.shutil actually performs recursive gzipping or bzipping of individual files in the directory, not creating a tar archive. Pls let me know if this is not what you meant.

2. Yes, TemporaryDirectory is indeed a complete replacement for ScratchDir. ScratchDir will be moved in future.

docker-py 1.5.0 specifies API version 1.20 as the default, but your docker daemon only supports up to API 1.19 (1.20 was introduced with Docker 1.8.x)

You can easily fix this by pinning the version you want to use when constructing the `Client`, i.e.

```
import docker
c = docker.Client(version='1.19')
```

Looks good from a code standpoint. I have a couple issues with the design, mainly with the idea of having the version be fetched on the first request. I feel like we're hiding what really happens behind the scenes and if issues happen during that phase it will confuse the hell out of users.

Additionally, I'm not sure this should be the default behavior (mainly because I dislike the idea of making that extra request in the general case).

What do you think of the following plan:

1. Add a special value "auto" to the valid `version` values, which will trigger this behavior
2. If `version=None`, use the `DEFAULT_DOCKER_API_VERSION` constant.
3. If `version="auto"`, fetch the API version from the daemon inside the `Client.__init__` method.

Alternatively, if you think having a request inside the `__init__` method is a bad idea, we could have an additional Client method called `retrieve_server_version()` or something similar that fetches the API version and sets the `Client._version` attribute. It's a bit extra work for the user but at least there's no implicit requests happening behind the scenes.
